title: Dog Limping After Heavy Exercise: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn why your dog's limp worsens after exercise, the urgency level for treatment, and how photos help your vet provide a faster diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: A dog limping after exercise often indicates an underlying musculoskeletal issue such as a ligament tear or joint inflammation and should be evaluated by a veterinarian within 24–48 hours. Providing a video of your dog’s gait at home is essential, as adrenaline can often mask the limp during a physical exam.

Why is my dog limping more after they exercise?

When a dog's limp becomes more pronounced following heavy exercise, it typically indicates an underlying musculoskeletal issue. This could range from a minor soft tissue strain or sprain to more significant conditions such as cranial cruciate ligament (CCL) tears, joint inflammation, or osteoarthritis. Because activity increases blood flow and stress on the affected limb, the pain and mechanical dysfunction become much more visible after the dog has been running or playing.

Is it a medical emergency if my dog starts limping after activity?

The urgency level for a limp that worsens with exercise is generally considered Medium. While it may not be a life-threatening emergency, it is a clear sign that your pet is experiencing pain and requires a veterinary examination within 24 to 48 hours. Seek immediate emergency care if the limp is accompanied by:

  • An inability to stand
  • Extreme lethargy
  • Visible bone deformity

Why should I take a photo or video of my dog's limp for the veterinarian?

  • Capturing a video of your dog walking or a photo of a specific area of swelling is invaluable for veterinary triage.
  • Dogs often experience a surge of adrenaline at the vet clinic which can temporarily mask their pain, making it difficult for the doctor to see the limp in person.
  • A clear video of the gait at home allows your veterinarian to analyze the specific movement pattern and determine which joint is likely affected before the physical exam even begins.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following heavy exercise, a dog may exhibit lameness, which can manifest as a shortened stride or head drop during weight bearing on the affected limb. Clinical evaluation should include palpation of bones, joints, and soft tissues from distal to proximal, noting any swelling, pain, instability, crepitation, reduced range of motion, or muscle atrophy. Serial examinations before and after exercise may be necessary for subtle or obscure cases. Radiography and other imaging techniques can be helpful to diagnose lameness.

Chapter: Orthopedics, Immunology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1198)

faqs.question: Should I let my dog 'walk it off' if they are limping?
faqs.answer: No. If your dog is limping, you should enforce strict rest and avoid further exercise until they have been cleared by a veterinarian to prevent further injury.

faqs.question: Can I give my dog over-the-counter human pain relief?
faqs.answer: No. Many human medications like Ibuprofen or Naproxen are highly toxic to dogs. Always consult your vet for pet-safe pain management.

faqs.question: How can I help my dog until the vet appointment?
faqs.answer: Keep your dog confined to a small area, use a leash for bathroom breaks to prevent running, and avoid having them jump on or off furniture.
